MBA (Data Science) Major Project · Amity University Online · eClerx Services, Pune

Forecasting the Google Cloud bill with 98.6% accuracy.

A complete, reproducible platform that turns six years of Google Cloud billing history into an accurate FY2026 budget forecast — using a four-model ensemble, honest blind-year validation, traffic-elasticity scenarios, and a forward-looking overspend monitor. All figures in Indian rupees.

What this project does

From raw billing data to a budget you can defend

Five connected capabilities, each building on the last, that move cloud financial management from reactive surprise to anticipatory control.

01 · UNDERSTAND

Decompose six years of spend

72 months across 12 service lines, with trend, seasonality and two structural breaks — the 2021 migration wave and the 2023 FinOps optimization — recovered explicitly.

02 · MODEL

Four models, one ensemble

SARIMA, Prophet, XGBoost and an LSTM, combined by inverse validation error so the most reliable models dominate the blend.

03 · VALIDATE

Prove it on a blind year

Trained on 2020–2024, the ensemble forecasts an unseen 2025 once — landing within 1.4% with full 99% interval coverage.

04 · FORECAST

Project FY2026 with uncertainty

A ₹26.53 Cr forward forecast, +34.7% year-on-year, every month bounded by a 99% prediction interval.

05 · DECIDE

Scenarios & budget alerts

A fitted cost-to-traffic elasticity drives four growth scenarios, and a budget monitor flags overspend months ahead.

+ · REPRODUCE

Auditable end to end

One seeded Colab notebook regenerates every figure and metric, so every claim can be verified and re-run on real billing data.

The headline result

An ensemble that forecasts the cloud bill on a year it never saw

Under a strict, leakage-free protocol — models trained on 2020–2023, weights chosen on a 2024 validation year, then re-fit and tested once on a fully blind 2025 — the weighted ensemble achieved a mean absolute percentage error of 1.40%, equivalent to 98.60% accuracy, with an R² of 0.956 and 100% coverage of its 99% prediction interval.

The two statistical models carried the ensemble while the data-hungry machine-learning models were automatically down-weighted — exactly what the forecasting literature predicts for a short business series, and a demonstration that honest weighting protects the forecast from its weakest members.

Full results & tables →

01 · The Business Problem

Why the cloud bill refuses to behave.

Cloud computing turned a fixed, predictable capital cost into a continuously metered operating cost — distributed to every engineer, yet still owned by finance. That mismatch is the root of the budgeting problem this project solves.

The problem statement

eClerx's monthly Google Cloud cost grew from roughly ₹38 lakh in early 2020 to about ₹19.69 Cr across calendar 2025 — a compound annual growth rate above 34.1%. This growth is neither smooth nor stationary: it carries a strong annual seasonal pattern, it was stepped up by a 2021 cloud-migration wave, and it was partially offset by a 2023 FinOps optimization.

Against this backdrop, the organization's habitual budgeting method — last year's spend plus a flat percentage — is structurally incapable of tracking the true trajectory. The result is widening budget variances and reactive, after-the-fact cost firefighting.

Why the heuristic fails

  • The growth rate itself changes year to year, so a fixed uplift is wrong almost every year.
  • The service mix shifts toward faster-growing services like Vertex AI, mechanically accelerating the aggregate rate.
  • Structural events — migrations, optimizations — break any simple extrapolation.
  • The heuristic gives no uncertainty, so finance cannot size a contingency buffer rationally.
  • It is reactive: a breach is discovered at month-end, not forecast months ahead.
Organizational context

eClerx Services Limited

eClerx is a publicly listed Indian knowledge-process and data-analytics company, headquartered in Mumbai with major delivery operations in Pune, serving clients in financial services, retail and technology. The nature of its business — large-scale data processing, analytics, digital operations and increasingly machine-learning-enabled services — makes it a heavy and rapidly growing consumer of cloud compute, storage, analytics and AI services. As its Google Cloud estate has expanded, the monthly bill has grown from a minor operating cost into a material, compounding expense that demands disciplined forecasting.

Research objectives

Five objectives, from data to decisions

The first three form the analytical core; the last two extend the work into decision support.

Characterize the spend. Collect and structure six years of GCP billing data (Jan 2020 – Dec 2025) across twelve service lines, and identify the trend, seasonal and structural patterns in the organization's cloud expenditure.

Build and combine four models. Develop SARIMA, Prophet, XGBoost-on-log-differences and an LSTM, then combine them into a validation-weighted ensemble that forecasts monthly spend accurately on a fully blind twelve-month hold-out.

Quantify uncertainty. Construct 99% prediction intervals and verify empirically that their realized coverage meets the nominal confidence level.

Operationalize for finance. Produce an FY2026 expenditure projection, fit a cost-to-traffic elasticity and simulate traffic-growth scenarios, and configure a budget-overspend monitor with explicit alert thresholds.

Communicate clearly. Present the complete pipeline through publication-quality, executive-ready visualizations and an interactive dashboard.

Hypotheses

Two questions, stated formally

H1. An ensemble of diverse models materially improves on the incumbent last-year-plus-fixed-percentage heuristic. Supported — the ensemble's 1.40% error is roughly 3–4× better.

H2. The 99% prediction intervals achieve their nominal coverage on unseen data. Supported — observed coverage was 100% on the test year.

02 · How It Works

A leakage-free pipeline, built to be trusted.

Every parameter, hyper-parameter and ensemble weight is fixed using data that strictly precedes the window it is judged on. That discipline is what makes the headline accuracy an honest estimate of real-world performance.

The five-stage flow

From billing data to decision support

01

Data

Daily synthetic GCP billing, 2020–2025, 12 service lines with migration, FinOps & anomaly events; resampled to 72 monthly points, modeled in log space.

02

Models

SARIMA(1,1,1)(1,1,1)₁₂ · Prophet (99% interval) · XGBoost on log-differences · LSTM(64) on a 12-month window.

03

Ensemble

Train 2020–23 → validate 2024 → inverse-squared-MAPE weights → re-fit 2020–24 → blind-test 2025 once.

04

Forecast

Re-fit on all six years, project FY2026 twelve months ahead with a 99% prediction interval.

05

Decisions

Cost-to-traffic elasticity scenarios and a budget-overspend monitor with green/amber/red thresholds.

The four models

Deliberately diverse, so their errors cancel

Model selection prized diversity of error structure over accumulation of similar techniques. Each spans a different forecasting tradition.

BOX–JENKINS

SARIMA

Order (1,1,1), seasonal (1,1,1)₁₂, fitted on log cost. Differencing absorbs the trend; seasonal terms capture the annual shape. The ensemble anchor — earns ~80% of the weight.

DECOMPOSITION

Prophet

Piecewise-linear trend with automatic changepoints, Fourier seasonality, 99% interval. Best single model in isolation, well suited to a series with structural breaks.

GRADIENT BOOSTING

XGBoost

Targets the first difference of log cost (to escape the extrapolation limit), on lagged differences, rolling means and calendar features, reconstructed recursively.

DEEP LEARNING

LSTM

A 64-unit recurrent network on a 12-month window. Included to span the deep-learning tradition and to test, honestly, how a data-hungry model behaves on a short series.

The evaluation protocol

Why a three-way split, not two

A two-way train-test split gives no clean way to choose ensemble weights without contaminating the test. The three-way split resolves this: the training window (2020–2023) fits each model; the validation year (2024), unseen during fitting, measures genuine out-of-sample accuracy and sets the weights; and the test year (2025), unseen during both, gives an uncontaminated final evaluation.

After validation, all four models are re-fit on 2020–2024 with weights frozen, then forecast 2025 — touched exactly once. No quantity derived from 2025 influences any modeling choice. This is what lets the 98.60% accuracy be called an honest deployment estimate.

Methodology at a glance

  • Data type: 864 service-month records, INR, log scale
  • Sample: 72 consecutive months, complete (no random sampling)
  • Sampling: purposive, strict calendar order
  • Tools: pandas, statsmodels, Prophet, XGBoost, TensorFlow/Keras, scikit-learn, SciPy
  • Metrics: MAPE, accuracy, RMSE, MAE, R², 99% interval coverage
  • Weights: inverse-squared 2024 validation MAPE
On the data. The billing series is a reproducible synthetic reconstruction (seed 20260607) calibrated to realistic GCP economics — a compounding trend, multiplicative seasonality, weekly and month-end patterns, the 2021 migration step, the 2023 optimization, plus noise and anomaly spikes. It stands in for confidential live billing, and the same pipeline re-estimates unchanged on a real BigQuery export. This choice is disclosed openly; re-verification on actual data is the first production step.
03 · Results & Forecast

The numbers, proven on a blind year.

Trained on 2020–2024 and tested once on an unseen 2025, the ensemble forecast the cloud bill to within 1.40% — then projected FY2026 at ₹26.53 Cr, with scenarios and a budget monitor built on top.

Model leaderboard

Blind hold-out performance, FY2025

The statistical pair leads; the machine-learning models are weaker on the short series and are down-weighted accordingly. The ensemble buys robustness, not a record.

ModelMAPEAccuracyR²RMSE
SARIMA1.62%98.38%0.9451₹3.0L
Prophet1.15%98.85%0.9654₹2.4L
XGBoost5.91%94.09%-0.0210₹12.9L
LSTM9.29%90.71%-0.7405₹16.9L
Ensemble (weighted)1.40%98.60%0.9563₹2.7L
Reading the hold-out

Every month inside the band

The ensemble forecast tracks the actuals closely across all twelve unseen months, and the 99% prediction interval contains every realized value — an empirical coverage of 100%. The mean absolute error is about ₹2.3 lakh per month on an average monthly spend near ₹1.64 Cr.

Residuals show no systematic drift across the year, confirming the ensemble captured the trend and seasonality rather than merely approximating them.

The forward forecast

FY2026: ₹26.53 Cr, up 34.7%

Re-fit on all six years, the ensemble projects the next twelve months with a 99% interval that widens modestly with horizon.

Traffic-growth scenarios

A log-log regression of cost on the traffic index fits an elasticity of 1.00 (r = 0.998), so cost scales almost one-for-one with traffic. Four scenarios — conservative, base, aggressive and peak — rebase the FY2026 total on that elasticity, giving principled rather than arbitrary what-if envelopes.

Budget overspend monitor

Each FY2026 month is scored against a threshold of ₹1.78 Cr (FY2025 average × 1.08). Because the estate grows ~34.7%, the whole year clears this average-based cap — the signal is to reset the budget envelope, not that an anomaly occurred. The monitor surfaces this 10–12 months ahead.

Honest note. The XGBoost and LSTM models post weak individual scores on this 72-month series — exactly the small-sample behaviour the literature predicts — and the inverse-error weighting correctly assigns them under 5% of the blend combined. The LSTM's score also varies slightly between runs; because it carries under 1% weight, the 98.60% ensemble result is stable regardless.

05 · References & Summary

References & project summary.

The literature this work builds on, a concise recap of the findings, and the reproducibility assets behind every number.

In one minute

Project summary

What was built

A reproducible platform that forecasts eClerx's monthly Google Cloud spend by combining four models — SARIMA, Prophet, XGBoost and an LSTM — into an inverse-error ensemble, validated under a strict leakage-free protocol and extended into FY2026 budgeting, elasticity scenarios and a budget-overspend monitor.

What it achieved

  • 98.60% accuracy (1.40% MAPE) on a blind FY2025 year
  • 100% coverage of the 99% prediction interval
  • ₹26.53 Cr FY2026 forecast (+34.7% YoY)
  • Cost-to-traffic elasticity of 1.00 (r = 0.998)

Key takeaways

Enterprise cloud cost only looks unpredictable — modeled with the right structure and combined honestly, it forecasts to within ~1.4%. On a short series the statistical models dominate and the ML models are rightly down-weighted, and the ensemble's value is robustness rather than a record score.

From study to production: swap the synthetic series for the real BigQuery billing export, re-verify accuracy and coverage, and run the pipeline on a quarterly re-forecast cadence.
Bibliography

Research papers

2010  Armbrust, M., Fox, A., Griffith, R., et al. (2010). A view of cloud computing. Communications of the ACM, 53(4), 50–58.
1969  Bates, J. M., & Granger, C. W. J. (1969). The combination of forecasts. Operational Research Quarterly, 20(4), 451–468.
1970  Box, G. E. P., & Jenkins, G. M. (1970). Time series analysis: Forecasting and control. Holden-Day.
1993  Chatfield, C. (1993). Calculating interval forecasts. Journal of Business & Economic Statistics, 11(2), 121–135.
2016  Chen, T., & Guestrin, C. (2016). XGBoost: A scalable tree boosting system. Proc. 22nd ACM SIGKDD, 785–794.
1989  Clemen, R. T. (1989). Combining forecasts: A review and annotated bibliography. Int. J. Forecasting, 5(4), 559–583.
1997  Hochreiter, S., & Schmidhuber, J. (1997). Long short-term memory. Neural Computation, 9(8), 1735–1780.
2006  Hyndman, R. J., & Koehler, A. B. (2006). Another look at measures of forecast accuracy. Int. J. Forecasting, 22(4), 679–688.
2020  Makridakis, S., Spiliotis, E., & Assimakopoulos, V. (2020). The M4 competition. Int. J. Forecasting, 36(1), 54–74.
2018  Taylor, S. J., & Letham, B. (2018). Forecasting at scale. The American Statistician, 72(1), 37–45.
2000  Tashman, L. J. (2000). Out-of-sample tests of forecasting accuracy. Int. J. Forecasting, 16(4), 437–450.

Reproducibility

Every number is auditable

The complete pipeline runs end to end in a single seeded Colab notebook on a free CPU runtime, regenerating every figure and metric on this site.

SEEDS

Fixed & documented

Seed 20260607 for the data generator, 42 for the models — identical results on any machine.

STACK

Open-source

pandas, statsmodels, Prophet, XGBoost, TensorFlow/Keras, scikit-learn, SciPy, Matplotlib.

SCOPE

72 months × 12 services

864 service-month records, Jan 2020 – Dec 2025, denominated in Indian rupees.

The Project

Cloud Budget Forecasting and Predictive Cost Analytics Platform for Google Cloud spend at eClerx — a four-model ensemble with blind hold-out validation, FY2026 forecasting, traffic-elasticity scenarios and a budget-overspend monitor.
